FHA 203k Loan Florida

FHA 203(k) renovation loans in Florida

Buy a Florida fixer-upper and finance the renovations in one loan. Here's how FHA 203(k) loans work, what they cover, and the difference between Limited and Standard.

Renovation Loans

FHA 203(k) renovation loans in Florida

An FHA 203(k) loan lets you finance the purchase (or refinance) of a home plus the cost of renovations in a single loan — ideal for Florida fixer-uppers, older homes, or properties that need work to meet FHA standards.

Two types of 203(k)

The Limited 203(k) covers non-structural repairs and cosmetic updates up to a set limit (no major structural work) — perfect for kitchens, baths, flooring, and systems. The Standard 203(k) handles larger or structural projects (additions, major systems, foundation) and requires a HUD consultant to oversee the work.

What it can cover

Eligible improvements include roofing, HVAC, plumbing and electrical, kitchens and baths, flooring, windows, accessibility upgrades, and (for Standard) structural and room additions. Luxury items like pools generally aren't covered.

Why it works in Florida

With older housing stock in many Florida markets and homes that may need updates to pass an FHA appraisal, a 203(k) lets you buy and renovate with one loan and one closing — still with the low FHA down payment.

Questions & Answers

Frequently asked questions

What is an FHA 203(k) loan?+
A renovation mortgage that combines the purchase (or refinance) of a home and the cost of eligible repairs or improvements into a single FHA loan with one closing.
What's the difference between Limited and Standard 203(k)?+
Limited 203(k) covers non-structural repairs up to a set limit; Standard 203(k) handles larger or structural projects and requires a HUD consultant.
What can an FHA 203(k) loan pay for?+
Roofing, HVAC, plumbing, electrical, kitchens, baths, flooring, windows, accessibility upgrades, and structural work under the Standard program. Luxury items like pools generally aren't eligible.
Can I use a 203(k) to renovate my current home?+
Yes — a 203(k) can be used to refinance and renovate a home you already own, not just for purchases.
